[[fr:Iseyama Kotai Jingu]][[ja:伊勢山皇大神宮]][[zh:伊勢山皇大神宮]] {{Expand Chinese}} {{Otheruses|{{ill|Kōtai Jingū (disambiguation)|ja|皇大神宮 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū (disambiguation)|qq=}}}} {{infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image=File:Iseyama Koutai Jingu.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Nishi-ku|qid=Q1203393|1=Nishi-ku, Yokohama}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.450278|N|139.626111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}}}} '''Iseyama Kōtai Jingū''' is a [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo shrine]]. It is located in {{ill|Yokohama|en|Yokohama|qid=Q38283|lt=Yokohama|1=Yokohama}} and worships [[Amaterasu]].{{Cite web |title=Iseyama Kotai Jingu Shrine - Destinations |url=https://trip.pref.kanagawa.jp/destination/iseyama-kotai-jingu-shrine/423 |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=Tokyo Day Trip |language=en}} It is the main shrine of {{ill|Yokohama|en|Yokohama|qid=Q38283|lt=Yokohama|1=Yokohama}}.{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-03-23|title=Iseyama Kotai Jingu (Yokohama)|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/03/23/iseyama-kotai-jingu-yokohama/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}{{Cite web|title=Iseyama Kotai Jingu Shrine - Destinations|url=https://trip.pref.kanagawa.jp/destination/iseyama-kotai-jingu-shrine/423|access-date=2024-03-29|website=Tokyo Day Trip|language=en}}. It was the first shrine in the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja honcho]] to ever go bankrupt. It was established in the century during the Meiji era reflecting the political changes of that time. The shrines history is intertwined with the dynamics of that era making it a site of significance for those interested in Japanese history. The exact beginnings of Iseyama Kotai Jingu are clouded in mystery with information about its original location. In 1869 during the period of the Meiji Restoration the shrine was moved to its location at the behest of the Meiji government. This relocation was influenced by factors including socio changes and concerns over expanding foreign settlements and Christianitys growing influence perceived as a threat to traditional Japanese values and sovereignty. The presence of missionaries ministering to foreigners in Yokohama under treaties enforced by powers posed a significant challenge to Japan. Christianity had been outlawed since 1614 due, to fears that it could undermine beliefs and authority. == History == === Counteraction Against Christianity === The arrival of Christian missionaries and their allowed ministry to the foreign populace in Yokohama, under treaties imposed by Western powers, was a significant concern for the Japanese government. Christianity had been banned since 1614, regarded by the Japanese as a potentially subversive ideology. In response to this perceived threat, the Meiji government promoted an emperor-centric version of Shintoism as the state religion. Iseyama Kotai Jingu, strategically positioned on a hill overlooking the foreign settlement, was established as part of this initiative. The shrine dedicated to Amaterasu Omikami, the ancestral deity of the imperial family and a paramount figure in the Shinto pantheon, symbolized a reaffirmation of national identity and resistance against foreign influence. === Political Symbolism and Spiritual Significance === The establishment of Iseyama Kotai Jingu was a deliberate political act, as acknowledged by the shrine's own historical accounts. It aimed to unify the nation, safeguard against foreign encroachments, curb the spread of Christianity, and foster loyalty to the emperor. The choice of Amaterasu Omikami as the shrine's deity was emblematic of these objectives. In 1871, a significant ceremony transferred the divided spirit (bunrei) of Amaterasu Omikami from Ise Jingu to Iseyama Kotai Jingu, reinforcing its spiritual and political significance. Remarkably, the shrine introduced a hereditary system for its chief priesthood, a move that seemed at odds with the broader Meiji government's efforts towards modernization and Westernization. == Modern times == The shrine was devastated by the Great Kanto Earthquake in 1923 but was rebuilt in 1928. In response, to the perceived threat the Meiji government endorsed a version of Shintoism centered around the emperor as the religion. Iseyama Kotai Jingu, strategically situated on a hill overlooking the settlement was established as part of this endeavor. The shrine dedicated to Amaterasu Omikami, the deity of the family and a significant figure in Shinto beliefs represented a reaffirmation of national identity and a stand, against foreign influences. Iseyama Kotai Jingu is well known today for its inclusive atmosphere acting as a hub, for both community gatherings and religious events. It is especially renowned for hatsumiyamairi a ceremony marking the shrine visit of newborns. Also serves as a popular venue for weddings due to its proximity to a spacious wedding reception hall. ==Jawiki translation== {{Multiple Issues | Citation Notice = February 16, 2022 (Wed) 06:17 (UTC) | Primary Sources = October 9, 2022 (Sun) 03:10 (UTC) | Promotion = October 9, 2022 (Sun) 03:10 (UTC) | Grandiose Claims = October 9, 2022 (Sun) 03:10 (UTC) | Original Research = October 9, 2022 (Sun) 03:10 (UTC) | Japanese Expression = October 9, 2022 (Sun) 03:10 (UTC) }} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = '''Iseyama Koutai-jingu''' (伊勢山皇大神宮) | image = [[File:Iseyama koutai-jingu Torii.jpg|270px|Iseyama Koutai-jingu]]
Torii and Haiden | Location = {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}} {{ill|Yokohama|en|Yokohama|qid=Q38283|lt=Yokohama|1=Yokohama}} Town|lt_ja=宮崎町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Town (Yokohama)|1=Miyazaki-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q48764663}}64 | Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|27|01|N|139|37|34|E|region:JP-14_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=伊勢山皇大神宮}} | Enshrined Deity = {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} | Shrine Rank = Former Kanpei-kokuhei Shosha, Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} | Founded = {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 3 ({{ill|1870|en|1870|qid=Q7741|lt=1870|1=1870}}) | Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=2018|1=2018}} ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=Heisei 30|1=2018}}) – relocated from the old Saibaden of the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} ({{ill|Kōtai-jingu|ja|皇大神宮|lt=Inner Shrine|lt_ja=内宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai-jingu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Kōtai Jingū|qid=Q11581011}})) | Alternate Names = {{ill|Kōtai-jingu|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai-jingu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Kōtai Jingū|qid=Q11581011}}・{{ill|Daijingū|ja|大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū|qq=}} | Annual Festival = {{ill|May 15|en|May 15|qid=Q2562|lt=May 15|1=May 15}} | Map = {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|marker=religious-shinto}} {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa#Japan|label=伊勢山皇大神宮||mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} }} '''Iseyama Koutai-jingu''' (いせやまこうたいじんぐう) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|qid=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Yokohama|en|Yokohama|qid=Q38283|lt=Yokohama|1=Yokohama}}, {{ill|Nishi-ku, Yokohama|en|Nishi-ku, Yokohama|qid=Q1203393|lt=Nishi Ward|1=Nishi-ku, Yokohama}}, and {{ill|Miyazaki Town (Yokohama)|ja|宮崎町 (横浜市)|lt=Miyazaki Town|lt_ja=宮崎町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Town (Yokohama)|1=Miyazaki-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q48764663}}. It enshrines {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|アマテラス|lt=Amaterasu|lt_ja=天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} as its deity, and the cherry blossom is its {{ill|Shrine Emblem|ja|社紋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine Emblem|1=Shamon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18337040}}. In Yokohama it is often referred to as “Kōtai-jingu”{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Its former shrine Rank was {{ill|Kanpei-kokuhei Shosha|ja|官幣国幣社等外別格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpei-kokuhei Shosha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, later becoming a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. It is also notable as the first shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|qid=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}} to have received a {{ill|bankruptcy declaration|ja|破産宣告|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bankruptcy declaration|1=bankruptcy declaration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11587613}}. == History == {{Citation Notice|section=1|date=August 4, 2022 (Thu) 07:06 (UTC)}} === Founding === The shrine was founded in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 3 ({{ill|1870|en|1870|qid=Q7741|lt=1870|1=1870}}). Yokohama, once a small village, rapidly developed into a trading city after its opening as a port. Kanagawa Prefecture considered the establishment of Shinto as a necessary spiritual pillar for a city increasingly exposed to foreign cultures such as {{ill|Christianity|en|Christianity|qid=Q5043|lt=Christianity|1=Christianity}}, among others{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Accordingly, the kokushi of {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|qid=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} is said to have, by {{ill|Imperial Command|ja|勅命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Command|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勅|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 勅|1=Command paper|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q905765}}, enshrined the deity from {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} (through the process of {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|qid=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement|1=Bunrei}}) from the hilltop Shinmei Shrine of Ise Forest on the coast of {{ill|Tobe Village|ja|戸部村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobe Village|1=Tobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18987968}}, and in April of the same year relocated it to its present site on Nogeyama, designating it as the guardian shrine of Yokohama. Regarding the choice of this site, the Iseyama Stele erected in {{ill|1874|en|1874|qid=Q7761|lt=1874|1=1874}} ({{ill|1874|en|1874|qid=Q7761|lt=Meiji 7|1=1874}}) states: “One can vividly see the bustling foreign trading houses and the towering masts of our nation’s ships lining the bay. In this age of {{ill|Bunmei-kaika|en|Bunmei-kaika|qid=Q700324|lt=Civilization and Enlightenment|1=Bunmei-kaika}}, if this place is developed and the divine spirit enshrined, the gods will surely accept our sincerity.” In November of the same year, the then Governor of Kanagawa Prefecture, {{ill|Morikata Isesei|ja|井関盛艮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morikata Isesei|1=Moritome Iseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11373804}}, submitted a petition to the {{ill|Daijō-kan (Meiji period)|ja|太政官 (明治時代)|lt=Daijō-kan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan (Meiji period)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125768223}} and the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|qid=Q2264720|lt=Jingi-kan|1=Department of Divinities}}{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. The petition requested that the shrine be designated as an observation site for {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} and developed on a scale befitting the principal shrine of the prefecture. The following month, permission was granted and it received a shrine Rank equivalent to the government and national shrines ({{ill|Kanpei-kokuhei Shosha|ja|官幣国幣社等外別格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpei-kokuhei Shosha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}){{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}, spurring the expansion of its precinct facilities. Later, similar observation sites were established in Kobe and Nagasaki, both open port cities{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. However, whereas those sites—as per document No.8 issued in March {{ill|1876|en|1876|qid=Q7766|lt=1876|1=1876}} ({{ill|1876|en|1876|qid=Q7766|lt=Meiji 9|1=1876}}) by the {{ill|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|en|Ministry of Religion (Japan)|qid=Q4294653|lt=Ministry of Religion|1=Ministry of Religion (Japan)}}—were merely “observation sites” lacking formal enshrinement, Iseyama Koutai-jingu was a full shrine where the spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} was enshrined. Hence, subsequent official documents did not refer to it as an observation site; rather, following the precedent of Ise Grand Shrine, it was called “Kōtai-jingu”{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. There are many examples of shrines enshrining {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照皇大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} that bear the names “Kōtai-jingu” or “Daijingū”, but in the case of Iseyama Koutai-jingu, since it was founded by the prefecture in conjunction with the imperial proclamation of the Great Teachings and Ranked by the Jingi-kan as equivalent to national shrines, it is close to an instance where the title “Jingū” was granted by imperial permission{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. === The Hill of Iseyama === The hill on which the shrine stands, known as Iseyama, was originally called Nogeyama; it was renamed Iseyama upon the relocation (although the place name {{ill|Ise Town (Yokohama)|ja|伊勢町 (横浜市)|lt=Ise Town|lt_ja=伊勢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Town (Yokohama)|1=Ise-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q48764600}} remains, the name Iseyama itself no longer exists). In former times, the lowlands of Miyazaki Town and {{ill|Hanasakicho (Yokohama)|ja|花咲町 (横浜市)|lt=Hanasakicho|lt_ja=花咲町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanasakicho (Yokohama)|1=Hanasaki-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11615173}} were part of the sea, and {{ill|Nogechō|ja|野毛町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nogechō|1=Nogechō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11645750}} was known as Nogeura, an inlet. Land reclamation in these areas began with the opening of the port in the late Edo period. Part of the shrine grounds contains an archaeological site known as the Iseyama Shell Mound, from which late Jomon period pottery has been excavated{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Moreover, Jomon-era dwelling sites have been discovered around areas such as Momiji-gaoka and Osamatsuchō{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Since present-day central Yokohama corresponds to Yoshida Shinden, reclaimed in the early Edo period, this entire hill area is considered one of the oldest residential regions in the vicinity of central Yokohama{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. In the sixth year of the Ansei era ({{ill|1859|en|1859|qid=Q7687|lt=1859|1=1859}}) immediately after the port’s opening, the {{ill|Kanagawa Bugyōsho|ja|神奈川奉行所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Bugyōsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神奈川奉行|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神奈川奉行|1=Kanagawa bugyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4262621}} was established on this hill. Its selection was due to its commanding view of both the port and the foreign settlement, as well as its strategic position from which the British and French troops—stationed on the hill in Yamate ({{ill|Yamate|en|Yamate|qid=Q8047957|lt=Yamate|1=Yamate}}) under the pretext of protecting residents—could be monitored. The entrance to the port at {{ill|Yoshida Bridge|ja|吉田橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Bridge|1=Yoshida Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22120821}} and the road linking to the Tokaido, {{ill|Yokohama Road|ja|横浜道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokohama Road|1=Yokohama Road|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11543269}}, were constructed through a passage (Nogekiridoshi) on this hill, running beside the Bugyōsho; this was also for defensive purposes. Subsequently, the Kanagawa Bugyōsho was taken over by the Meiji government, with its functions divided between {{ill|Yokohama Customs|ja|横浜税関|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokohama Customs|1=Yokohama Customs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11121610}} and the Yokohama District Court—the latter later becoming the precursor to the {{ill|Kanagawa Prefectural Government|ja|神奈川県庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Prefectural Government|1=Kanagawa Prefectural Government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11588975}}. Later, as mentioned above, because the site offered a commanding view of both the port and central Yokohama, it was deemed an ideal location for a shrine, leading to its establishment{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. === Relocation Festival === During the relocation festival held on April 15 (lunar calendar) in {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 3 ({{ill|1870|en|1870|qid=Q7741|lt=1870|1=1870}}), an edict was issued by Kanagawa Prefecture calling for a grand, citywide celebration over five days{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. In response, various towns within the city organized their own events—with puppet floats, traditional hand dances, and mobile dance processions—parading through central Yokohama in areas such as {{ill|Motomachi (Yokohama)|ja|本町 (横浜市)|lt=Motomachi|lt_ja=本町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motomachi (Yokohama)|1=Honchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28682962}}, {{ill|Benten-dori|ja|弁天通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benten-dori|1=Benten-dōri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28682961}}, and {{ill|Bashamichi (Yokohama)|ja|馬車道 (横浜市)|lt=Bashamichi|lt_ja=馬車道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashamichi (Yokohama)|1=Bashamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11668080}}, competing in lavish display{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. In front of the prefectural office, viewing stands were set up for foreign envoys and consuls, and records indicate that many spectators from Tokyo also flocked to witness the festivities{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Notably, in the four districts of Motomachi—a thriving trading district at the time—orders for puppet floats were placed with {{ill|Nakahidei|ja|仲秀英|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakahidei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, reputed as Edo’s foremost float artisan. According to the official "Yokohama City Historical Records" (published in {{ill|1920|en|1920|qid=Q2155|lt=1920|1=1920}} (Taishō 9)){{要ページ番号|date=October 2022}}, these four floats were “the epitome of artistic craftsmanship, executed at great expense.” Although they were destroyed in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kantō Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3742341}}, Nakahidei’s surviving floats in places such as Kawagoe have been designated as prefectural cultural properties and are presumed to have received commensurate acclaim had they survived{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. It is said that the total cost of this grand festival ranged between 100,000 and 150,000 ryo—including a subsidy of 5,000 ryo from the prefecture. Legend has it that when the hostess of the renowned restaurant “Fukirō Okura” (a representative establishment in Yokohama) {{ill|Fukirō Okura|ja|富貴楼お倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukirō Okura|1=Fukiro Okura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11457319}} related the story to Foreign Minister {{ill|Soejima Taneomi|en|Soejima Taneomi|qid=Q702789|lt=Sokushima Tanetomi|1=Soejima Taneomi}}, he reportedly exclaimed, “That amount is equivalent to half a year’s budget for the Ministry of Foreign Affairs!”{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Moreover, a silk merchant residing in Yokohama once wrote in a letter to his hometown about the festival’s magnificence and bustling atmosphere, noting that “it demonstrated to both domestic and international audiences that Yokohama had become Japan’s foremost city”{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Another reason for the festival was that most Yokohama residents at the time were migrants from other regions, resulting in weak neighborhood ties and a diminished sense of community{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. In a letter sent in the first year of Meiji by a clerk for a Yokohama silk merchant, it was written, “Yokohama is smaller than Edo, yet often you don’t even know who your neighbors are. It’s convenient not having to socialize.”{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}} This was highly unusual for the time and not an ideal state from the prefectural perspective. Thus, the festival’s exuberance was used as a catalyst to unite the residents and instill a sense of “{{ill|Citizenship|en|Citizenship|qid=Q42138|lt=Citizens|1=Citizenship}}” among them{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Among Yokohama’s residents, the self-conscious spirit known as “Hamacco” is often noted, and one origin of this identity is attributed to the relocation festival of Iseyama Koutai-jingu{{誰2|date=February 2022}}. Note that although the relocation was originally held on April 14 (lunar calendar) and designated as the annual festival day, the adoption of the {{ill|Solar calendar|en|Solar calendar|qid=Q210953|lt=Solar calendar|1=Solar calendar}} in {{ill|1873|en|1873|qid=Q7757|lt=1873|1=1873}} ({{ill|1873|en|1873|qid=Q7757|lt=Meiji 6|1=1873}}) shifted the festival day to May 15—a “month-late” date. Until the end of {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=World War II|1=World War II}}, this day was observed as a public holiday in Yokohama, with schools, government offices, and factories closed{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. === Iseyama Detached Palace === {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} frequently visited Yokohama—for naval inspections (until {{ill|1884|en|1884|qid=Q7819|lt=1884|1=1884}}, when the Imperial Japanese Navy’s Eastern Sea {{ill|Chinjufu (Imperial Japanese Navy)|ja|鎮守府 (日本海軍)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinjufu (Imperial Japanese Navy)|1=Naval District|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11651162}} was based at Yokohama Port) and for daytime horse races at {{ill|Negishi Racecourse|ja|根岸競馬場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Negishi Racecourse|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 横浜競馬場|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 横浜競馬場|1=Negishi Racecourse|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11543239}}, a social venue for foreign consuls rivaling {{ill|Rokumeikan|en|Rokumeikan|qid=Q1039219|lt=Rokumeikan|1=Rokumeikan}}—and thus visited frequently. Consequently, in {{ill|1875|en|1875|qid=Q7764|lt=1875|1=1875}} ({{ill|1875|en|1875|qid=Q7764|lt=Meiji 8|1=1875}}), a two‐story Western-style building designated as the reception hall during his visits was constructed adjacent to Iseyama Koutai-jingu. This building, known as the Yokohama {{ill|Official Residence|ja|御用邸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Official Residence|1=Imperial villa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q67097832}} – Iseyama Detached Palace, served as his temporary sitting chamber. In {{ill|1876|en|1876|qid=Q7766|lt=1876|1=1876}} ({{ill|1876|en|1876|qid=Q7766|lt=Meiji 9|1=1876}}), Emperor Meiji embarked on an inspection tour of the Tōhoku region; at the tour’s conclusion he sailed to Hokkaido, departing from Hakodate Port on the imperial yacht {{ill|Meiji Maru|en|Meiji Maru|qid=Q11512300|lt=Meiji Maru|1=Meiji Maru}}, bound for Yokohama Port. Although his arrival was scheduled for the morning of July 20, inclement weather delayed his arrival until late at night. Ultimately, with the reception for foreign consuls abruptly canceled, he took refuge and spent the night at Iseyama Detached Palace. Incidentally, based on this anecdote, July 20 was designated as Marine Day in {{ill|1941|en|1941|qid=Q5231|lt=1941|1=1941}} ({{ill|1941|en|1941|qid=Q5231|lt=Showa 16|1=1941}}), and from {{ill|1995|en|1995|qid=Q2068|lt=1995|1=1995}} ({{ill|1995|en|1995|qid=Q2068|lt=Heisei 7|1=1995}}) onward, it has been observed as the national holiday {{ill|Marine Day|en|Marine Day|qid=Q1355833|lt=Marine Day|1=Marine Day}}. Furthermore, Iseyama Detached Palace was also used as a {{ill|State guest house|en|State guest house|qid=Q52843464|lt=Reception Hall|1=State guest house}}, and it hosted, among others, King {{ill|Kalākaua|en|Kalākaua|qid=Q163080|lt=Kalākaua|1=Kalākaua}} of the {{ill|Hawaiian Kingdom|en|Hawaiian Kingdom|qid=Q156418|lt=Kingdom of Hawaii|1=Hawaiian Kingdom}}, the first state guest to visit Japan under the Meiji government, as well as Admiral Pasha—the goodwill envoy of {{ill|Ottoman Turkey|ja|オスマントルコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ottoman Turkey|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オスマン帝国|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オスマン帝国|1=Ottoman Empire|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q12560}}—who perished when his vessel was shipwrecked off the coast of Kushimoto, Wakayama Prefecture (an event known as the {{ill|Ertuğrul Incident|ja|エルトゥールル号遭難事件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ertuğrul Incident|1=sinking of the Ertuğrul|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11291067}}). === Relationship with Kanagawa Prefecture === Iseyama Koutai-jingu was founded by the prefectural governor, {{ill|Morikata Isesei|ja|井関盛艮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morikata Isesei|1=Moritome Iseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11373804}}, and by virtue of his petition, was designated by the state as the official religious shrine of Kanagawa Prefecture{{Cite book|和書|author=Yokohama City |title=Yokohama City Historical Records |publisher=Rinkawasen Publishing |year=1986 |pages=46-47 |isbn=}}{{Full|date=October 2022}}. Furthermore, the hill of Iseyama is the former site of the {{ill|Kanagawa Bugyōsho|ja|神奈川奉行所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Bugyōsho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神奈川奉行|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神奈川奉行|1=Kanagawa bugyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4262621}}, the predecessor of the prefectural government. From its inception the shrine maintained a close relationship with the prefecture—a bond that has endured. Upon the inauguration or departure of a prefectural governor, it became customary to visit the shrine and hold a ceremonial ritual; indeed, during the May annual festival, it was not uncommon for the governor to personally don ceremonial attire and serve as the priest ({{ill|Hōkaku Kyōshinshi|ja|幣帛供進使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkaku Kyōshinshi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 奉幣|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 奉幣|1=Hōhei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11445508}}) without appointing a proxy{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Moreover, although no {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}} was established in Kanagawa, Iseyama assumed that role; in autumn, memorial ceremonies for war casualties—dating back to the {{ill|Satsuma Rebellion|en|Satsuma Rebellion|qid=Q714820|lt=Satsuma Rebellion|1=Satsuma Rebellion}}—were held by the prefecture (as noted in “Yokohama Enkaku-shi”{{要ページ番号|date=October 2022}}). For this reason, it was sometimes also known by the alternate name “Nogeyama {{ill|Shōkonsha|ja|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōkonsha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Shōkonsha|qid=Q11497810}}”. During the Taishō period, memorial services for fallen prefectural police officers and firefighters were held, and fencing competitions were organized as part of the dedication events. In wartime, the prefecture frequently sponsored victory prayer and proclamation ceremonies{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Thus, it once functioned very strongly as the official shrine of the prefecture. === Rebuilding after Bankruptcy === In {{ill|1991|en|1991|qid=Q2062|lt=1991|1=1991}} ({{ill|1991 in Japan|ja|1991st year の日本|lt=Heisei 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1991 in Japan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), {{ill|Religious Corporation|ja|宗教法人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Religious Corporation|1=religious corporation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7311343}} Iseyama Koutai-jingu constructed a luxury hotel called “Yokohama Kaiyōtei” on the land adjacent to the shrine’s back approach—a facility that also served as a wedding venue. To finance the hotel’s construction and the acquisition of the land, approximately 8.7 billion yen was borrowed using part of the shrine grounds as collateral (including 4.3 billion yen from {{ill|Yasuda / Fuji Bank|en|Yasuda / Fuji Bank|qid=Q4118927|lt=Fuji Bank|1=Yasuda / Fuji Bank}}, 1.75 billion yen from {{ill|Hokuriku Bank|en|Hokuriku Bank|qid=Q5878308|lt=Hokuriku Bank|1=Hokuriku Bank}}, 1.5 billion yen from {{ill|Mitsubishi UFJ Trust and Banking Corporation|en|Mitsubishi UFJ Trust and Banking Corporation|qid=Q6883178|lt=Mitsubishi Trust Bank|1=Mitsubishi UFJ Trust and Banking Corporation}}, 1.4 billion yen from {{ill|Mitsubishi Bank|en|Mitsubishi Bank|qid=Q11357162|lt=Mitsubishi Bank|1=Mitsubishi Bank}}, among others). The hotel was operated by a separate corporation, Kaiyōtei Corporation, which was managed by relatives of the shrine’s then-{{ill|Head Priest|ja|宮司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Head Priest|1=Gūji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11453500}}, who owned the majority of its {{ill|Share (finance)|en|Share (finance)|qid=Q11692|lt=shares|1=Share (finance)}}. The Religious Corporation Iseyama Koutai-jingu planned to lease the hotel to this operating company and use the rental income to repay its debts. However, following the burst of the bubble and the subsequent economic downturn, as well as the abolition of the section of the {{ill|Tokyu Toyoko Line|ja|東横線|lt=Toyoko Line|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyu Toyoko Line|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東急東横線|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東急東横線|1=Tōyoko Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1192468}} between {{ill|Yokohama Station|en|Yokohama Station|qid=Q801658|lt=Yokohama Station|1=Yokohama Station}} and {{ill|Sakuragichō Station|en|Sakuragichō Station|qid=Q801396|lt=Sakuragicho Station|1=Sakuragichō Station}} (later reconnected to the Minato Mirai area’s rail network), customer traffic shifted to nearby areas such as the {{ill|Minato Mirai 21|en|Minato Mirai 21|qid=Q1140617|lt=Yokohama Minato Mirai 21|1=Minato Mirai 21}} district. Coupled with a decline in Shinto wedding ceremonies, the hotel’s operations faltered and the expected rental income did not materialize. Consequently, the repayment of the borrowed funds became unfeasible, and in March {{ill|2001|en|2001|qid=Q1988|lt=2001|1=2001}} ({{ill|2001|en|2001|qid=Q1988|lt=Heisei 13|1=2001}}) a portion of the shrine grounds—already encumbered by a {{ill|Hypothec|en|Hypothec|qid=Q184260|lt=Mortgage|1=Hypothec}}—was seized by financial institutions through a {{ill|seizure|ja|差押|lt=Seizure|lt_ja=差し押さえ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seizure|1=Garnishment|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q863668}}. In {{ill|2002|en|2002|qid=Q1987|lt=2002|1=2002}} ({{ill|2002|en|2002|qid=Q1987|lt=Heisei 14|1=2002}}), as the situation worsened, the {{ill|Kanagawa Jinja-chō|ja|神社庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Jinja-chō|1=Jinjachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11590703}} of Kanagawa Prefecture, after negotiations with the banks, acquired the portion of the shrine grounds excluding the hotel site and assumed direct control of the shrine’s operations, thereby averting an {{ill|Auction|en|Auction|qid=Q177923|lt=auction|1=Auction}}. The head priest was dismissed, and the hereditary priestly family—de facto in place since the Meiji period—was estranged from the shrine. Separated from its shrine management, the Religious Corporation Iseyama Koutai-jingu essentially became a real estate management company; however, as the asset value of the hotel portion plummeted and enormous debts mounted, it filed for {{ill|Bankruptcy|en|Bankruptcy|qid=Q152074|lt=Self-bankruptcy|1=Bankruptcy}} at the {{ill|Yokohama District Court|ja|横浜地方裁判所|lt=Yokohama District Court|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokohama District Court|1=Yokohama District Court|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11542961}}, and on {{ill|April 7|en|April 7|qid=Q2505|lt=April 7|1=April 7}} in {{ill|2003|en|2003|qid=Q1986|lt=2003|1=2003}} ({{ill|2003|en|2003|qid=Q1986|lt=Heisei 15|1=2003}}) it received a {{ill|bankruptcy declaration|ja|破産宣告|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bankruptcy declaration|1=bankruptcy declaration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11587613}}. Its liabilities amounted to 8.565 billion yen owed to two creditor companies, marking the first bankruptcy declared by a shrine belonging to the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|qid=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. The hotel (operated by Kaiyōtei Corporation) received a bankruptcy declaration on November 27, {{ill|2006|en|2006|qid=Q2021|lt=2006|1=2006}} ({{ill|2006|en|2006|qid=Q2021|lt=Heisei 18|1=2006}}) and ceased operations. Subsequently, under a new management system, reconstruction proceeded; it is now independent from direct control by the Kanagawa Jinja-chō, having regained its status as a religious corporation and been reinstated as a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|qid=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}}. On May 15, {{ill|2020|en|2020|qid=Q25337|lt=2020|1=2020}} ({{ill|2020|en|2020|qid=Q25337|lt=Reiwa 2|1=2020}} in Japan), the shrine celebrated its 150th anniversary. == Anecdotes == {{Citation Notice|section=1|date=August 4, 2022 (Thu) 07:06 (UTC)}} === Name === Due to ambiguities in reading segmentation—and because the character “皇” (from {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}}) is used—many mistakenly read “皇” as “nō,” misinterpreting the name as “ise '''san-nō-dai''' jingu.” In fact, some informational materials have circulated with such incorrect ruby annotations{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. === Cherry Blossoms === In the past, the shrine was renowned as one of Yokohama’s premier cherry blossom spots, with numerous cherry trees planted along the approach from the avenue continuing from {{ill|Kōyōzaka (Yokohama)|ja|紅葉坂 (横浜市)|lt=Kōyōzaka|lt_ja=紅葉坂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōyōzaka (Yokohama)|1=Momiji-zaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11543241}} to the shrine grounds[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AEBqI1zKP3U Old Postcards of Japan: YOKOHAMA]{{信頼性要検証|date=October 2022|投稿者の素性が不明なWeb動画}}. This is why cherry blossoms are featured in its emblem. According to the official "Yokohama City Historical Records" ({{ill|1920|en|1920|qid=Q2155|lt=1920|1=1920}} (Taishō 9)), the sight of cherry blossoms “like a crimson curtain in full bloom” along with the “grand torii floating among the blossoms” was eagerly anticipated by incoming ships, and it was noted that even foreigners praised its name. Although the cherry-lined avenue has since disappeared, the cherry trees remaining in the shrine grounds continue to be cherished as a symbol of the shrine by local residents. === Ise Grand Shrine's Branch Shrine === In {{ill|1875|en|1875|qid=Q7764|lt=1875|1=1875}} ({{ill|1875|en|1875|qid=Q7764|lt=Meiji 8|1=1875}}), Kanagawa Prefecture petitioned the {{ill|Daijō-kan (Meiji period)|ja|太政官 (明治時代)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan (Meiji period)|1=Dajōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125768223}} to elevate Iseyama Koutai-jingu to a branch shrine ({{ill|Betsukyū|ja|別宮|lt=Betsugū|1=Setsumatsusha|2=ja|3=摂末社|4=zh|5=攝末社|6=es|7=Setsumatsusha|8=fr|9=Setsumatsusha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betsukyū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} (see {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine#Overview|ja|伊勢神宮#概要|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine#Overview|qq=}}). Such an elevation had not been seen since the Kamakura period during the [[Mongol Invasions]] when shrines such as {{ill|Fūsokigū|ja|風日祈宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fūsokigū|1=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11665488}} and {{ill|Kazemiyashiro|ja|風宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazemiyashiro|1=Kaze-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11665463}} existed, and after deliberation the petition was rejected on June 28 (as recorded in the Taisei Ruitei). However, the "Yokohama Iseyama Stele," erected in January {{ill|1874|en|1874|qid=Q7761|lt=1874|1=1874}} ({{ill|1874|en|1874|qid=Q7761|lt=Meiji 7|1=1874}}) along the approach by Kanagawa Prefecture bears the inscription “Branch Shrine,” and the full text of the inscription was published as an appendix in issue 968 of the {{ill|Yokohama Mainichi Shimbun|ja|横浜毎日新聞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokohama Mainichi Shimbun|1=Yokohama Mainichi Shimbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11543205}} on February 23 of the same year. This stele still stands beside the large torii. === King’s Tower === The Kanagawa Prefectural Office was damaged in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kantō Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3742341}}, and in {{ill|1928|en|1928|qid=Q19715|lt=1928|1=1928}} ({{ill|1928|en|1928|qid=Q19715|lt=Showa 3|1=1928}}) the current main building of the {{ill|Kanagawa Prefectural Government Main Building|ja|神奈川県庁本庁舎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanagawa Prefectural Government Main Building|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神奈川県庁舎|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神奈川県庁舎|1=Kanagawa Prefectural offices|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10445111}} was completed. At its center stands a tower known as the “Cultivation Tower,” whose top floor houses an altar where a portion of the divine spirit ({{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|qid=Q195793|lt=Bunrei|1=Bunrei}}) from Iseyama Koutai-jingu was enshrined{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Every month, the shrine’s priests would visit this site to conduct rituals as the guardian deity of Yokohama Port{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Today, the building is designated as a {{ill|Registered Tangible Cultural Property|ja|登録有形文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Registered Tangible Cultural Property|1=Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11579194}}, and the Cultivation Tower is affectionately known as “King’s Tower,” being counted among the {{ill|Yokohama Three Towers|en|Yokohama Three Towers|qid=Q6135572|lt=Yokohama Three Towers|1=Yokohama Three Towers}}, symbols of Yokohama. === Yokohama Prints === From the late Edo to the early Meiji period, a style of woodblock prints known as {{ill|Yokohama-e|en|Yokohama-e|qid=Q3074667|lt=Yokohama-e|1=Yokohama-e}}—depicting the customs of Yokohama’s foreigners and the spirit of {{ill|Bunmei-kaika|en|Bunmei-kaika|qid=Q700324|lt=Civilization and Enlightenment|1=Bunmei-kaika}}—became popular. Several such prints feature Iseyama Koutai-jingu. A notable example is by the third-generation artist {{ill|Hiroshige|en|Hiroshige|qid=Q200798|lt=Utagawa Hiroshige|1=Hiroshige}}, who produced a print titled “Yokohama Nogei Iseyama Zyuukai Railway Steam Locomotive Picture” depicting a steam locomotive set against a backdrop of cherry blossoms at the shrine. This print has also been used in the shrine’s seal book. === Ice Cream === The first ice cream shop in Japan was opened in June of {{ill|1869|en|1869|qid=Q7720|lt=Meiji 2|1=1869}} ({{ill|1869|en|1869|qid=Q7720|lt=1869|1=1869}}) by {{ill|Machida Fūsō|ja|町田房蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machida Fūsō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Bashamichi (Yokohama)|ja|馬車道 (横浜市)|lt=Bashamichi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashamichi (Yokohama)|1=Bashamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11668080}}. However, as Japanese people at the time were not very familiar with dairy products—and with a price of 2 mon per serving (roughly 8,000 yen in today’s value)—the business initially did not succeed. According to “Yokohama Enkaku-shi” (published in Meiji 25), the shop temporarily closed due to losses, but when it reopened in April of the following year (Meiji 3) to coincide with the relocation festival of Iseyama Koutai-jingu, the novelty of ice cream amid the festival’s excitement led to a surge in demand, and the shop subsequently prospered. This event is credited with introducing ice cream to Japan{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}{{独自研究?|date=August 2022}}, later leading to its adoption at venues such as {{ill|Rokumeikan|en|Rokumeikan|qid=Q1039219|lt=Rokumeikan|1=Rokumeikan}} and Western-style confectionery shops in Tokyo. === Yokohama Naval Air Corps === {{ill|Yokohama Air Group|en|Yokohama Air Group|qid=Q8054612|lt=Yokohama Naval Air Corps|1=Yokohama Air Group}} was the largest seaplane unit of the {{ill|Imperial Japanese Navy|ja|帝国海軍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Japanese Navy|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大日本帝国海軍|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大日本帝国海軍|1=Imperial Japanese Navy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q219712}}, established in {{ill|1938|en|1938|qid=Q18645|lt=1938|1=1938}} ({{ill|1937|en|1937|qid=Q18647|lt=Showa 12|1=1937}}). Composed of large seaplanes boasting long-range capabilities, it was tasked with patrolling and reconnaissance over vast stretches of open ocean from the {{ill|Aleutian Islands|en|Aleutian Islands|qid=Q119285|lt=Aleutian Islands|1=Aleutian Islands}} to the {{ill|South Seas Mandate|en|South Seas Mandate|qid=Q718763|lt=South Seas Islands|1=South Seas Mandate}}. The unit regarded Iseyama Koutai-jingu as its guardian deity, transferring a portion of its divine spirit to a base at {{ill|Tomioka General Park|ja|富岡総合公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomioka General Park|1=Tomioka Comprehensive Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11456913}} in present-day Kanazawa Ward, where it was enshrined under the name {{ill|Torifune Shrine|ja|鳥船神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torifune Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579250|1=Torifune Shrine}}{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Its main aircraft, the {{ill|Kawanishi H8K|en|Kawanishi H8K|qid=Q696220|lt=Type 2 Seaplane|1=Kawanishi H8K}}, was renowned for its world-class performance—its speed, firepower, and defense earning it the moniker “floating battleship” from the Allied forces{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. A famous account recounts how, after World War II, the American military conducted performance tests on captured aircraft and awarded them the highest evaluation{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. Today, the {{ill|ShinMaywa US-2|en|ShinMaywa US-2|qid=Q1138466|lt=US-2|1=ShinMaywa US-2}} rescue seaplane, deployed by the {{ill|Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force|en|Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force|qid=Q731647|lt=Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force|1=Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force}}, is a direct descendant of the Type 2 Seaplane and is celebrated as a masterpiece of world-class performance{{独自研究?|date=August 2022}}. Although {{ill|Torifune Shrine|ja|鳥船神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Torifune Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579250|1=Torifune Shrine}} was effectively abolished following the U.S. military’s postwar seizure of military bases, when it was returned in {{ill|1971|en|1971|qid=Q2475|lt=1971|1=1971}} ({{ill|1971|en|1971|qid=Q2475|lt=Showa 46|1=1971}}), the veterans’ association “Hamakūkai” ({{ill|Veterans' Association|ja|戦友会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Veterans' Association|1=Veterans' Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11495987}} “Hamakūkai”) repaired the damaged shrine and re-enshrined the fallen under the name “Hamakū Shrine,” holding memorial ceremonies. Eventually, as the association aged and maintenance became difficult, in {{ill|2008|en|2008|qid=Q2004|lt=2008|1=2008}} ({{ill|2008|en|2008|qid=Q2004|lt=Heisei 20|1=2008}}) the shrine building was relocated to {{ill|Raijin Shrine (Yokosuka)|ja|雷神社 (横須賀市)|lt=Raijin Shrine|lt_ja=雷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine (Yokosuka)|1=Ikazuchi Shrine(Yokosuka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11660191}} in Yokosuka, and on the former site a memorial monument inscribed “In Remembrance – Naval Seaplane Unit” was erected. == Shrine Buildings and Grounds == {{Citation Notice|section=1|date=August 2022}} [[File:Iseyama koutai-jingu Honden.jpg|thumb|300x300px|Main Hall]] * Main Hall: A thatched {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|en|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} structure. In {{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=2018|1=2018}} ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=Heisei 30|1=2018}}), as part of the 150th-anniversary commemorative project, the old Saibaden from the Inner Shrine of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} ({{ill|Kōtai-jingu|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai-jingu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|comment=enwiki is a redirect|1=Kōtai Jingū|qid=Q11581011}}) was transferred and reconstructed in its original form as it stood in Ise. It is exceedingly rare for such an important shrine building to be relocated intact, and the permission to reproduce a thatched roof in an urban area is an exceptional case{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. * Setsumatsusha ** Kizuki Shrine (きづきのみや): Enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} as its main deity, along with {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|qid=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}, and also transfers the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-Omikami|1=Toyouke-hime}}—the deity of the Outer Shrine of Ise Grand Shrine. ** Konoodai no Kami (子之大神): The tutelary deity of {{ill|Nogechō|ja|野毛町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nogechō|1=Nogechō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11645750}}, enshrining {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Ubahime. Originally located within Nogeyama, its shrine building was lost to war, and it is now enshrined together with Kizuki Shrine. Ubahime is said to have been a goddess enshrined on “Ubaiwa” (a rock in the sea near {{ill|Sakuragichō Station|en|Sakuragichō Station|qid=Q801396|lt=Sakuragicho Station|1=Sakuragichō Station}} before land reclamation) and is traditionally believed to have been the wet nurse of {{ill|Prince Moriyoshi|en|Prince Moriyoshi|qid=Q2288858|lt=Goryō Shinnō|1=Prince Moriyoshi}}{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. ** {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|qid=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura|1=Iwakura rock}}: Enshrines {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}. It received a bunrei from the Miwa Myōjin of {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} in Nara and was enshrined in {{ill|1996|en|1996|qid=Q2070|lt=1996|1=1996}} ({{ill|1996|en|1996|qid=Q2070|lt=Heisei 8|1=1996}}). The shrine reproduces the ancient ritual site, “Iwakura,” that existed before the establishment of a formal shrine building. * Shōshikai (照四海): A perpetual lantern approximately 6 m tall; the current one is the second generation. True to its name “Illuminating the Four Seas,” it symbolizes the guardian deity of Yokohama Port. The first lantern, completed in {{ill|1882|en|1882|qid=Q7812|lt=1882|1=1882}} ({{ill|1882|en|1882|qid=Q7812|lt=Meiji 15|1=1882}}), resembled an early lighthouse and its light served as a navigational aid{{Citation needed|date=October 2022}}. * Former Torii Base: The base of a large torii from the Meiji period that was placed along the approach. It bears benchmark markings recorded when Yokohama’s map was drawn in {{ill|1876|en|1876|qid=Q7766|lt=1876|1=1876}} ({{ill|1876|en|1876|qid=Q7766|lt=Meiji 9|1=1876}}). * The Great Relocation (Daisen'gyo) Painting: In the antechamber “Sen'gyo no Ma” of the Kagura Hall (the indoor shrine), a large painting depicting the relocation ceremony (the ritual, as performed every 20 years at Ise Grand Shrine’s Shikinen Sengu, of transferring the sacred object to a new shrine) is displayed. Painted by the master Shinto artist Tori Rei, it measures 5 m in height and 18 m in width, making it one of the largest works in Japanese painting history{{Citation needed|date=October 2022}}. == Festivals and Annual Events == * Annual Festival: Held over three days—with the main festival on May 15 and preceding and following festivities—its origin dates back to the founding of the shrine on April 14 (lunar calendar) in Meiji 3. * Ōmiwa Shrine Iwakura Annual Festival: Held on April 9. * Kizuki Shrine and Konoodai no Kami Annual Festival: A summer festival held on August 20, during which, biennially, seven {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|qid=Q979940|lt=mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}} from various local parish associations converge for a joint procession. * Setsubun Festival: Held on February 3, featuring the custom of throwing roasted soybeans. * Ōharae (Great Purification): Rituals held on June 30 and December 31 to expunge sins and impurities. The June purification is known as “Natsugoshi Ōharae” (Summer Purification), during which a ritual of circling a large bundle of reeds is performed within the shrine grounds. * It is one of the earliest in Kanagawa Prefecture to hold Shinto wedding ceremonies, having commenced in {{ill|1902|en|1902|qid=Q2043|lt=1902|1=1902}} ({{ill|1902|en|1902|qid=Q2043|lt=Meiji 35|1=1902}}){{Citation needed|date=October 2022}}. == Devotees' Association == Since Iseyama Koutai-jingu does not have a designated parish area, it does not have a parish association; instead, a Devotees’ Association (formerly known as the Supporters’ Association) is organized by its worshippers. It is said that Princess Taikyu Sōko ({{ill|Naishinnō|ja|内親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishinnō|1=Imperial princess|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8015665}}, also known as {{ill|Toshiko Higashikuni|en|Toshiko Higashikuni|qid=Q7827709|lt=Tōkunin Sōko|1=Toshiko Higashikuni}}), the wife of {{ill|Prince Naruhiko Higashikuni|en|Prince Naruhiko Higashikuni|qid=Q315717|lt=Higashikuni no Miya Tadayoshi|1=Prince Naruhiko Higashikuni}}, once served as its president{{Citation needed|date=August 2022}}. == Works Set at Iseyama Koutai-jingu == * ''Yokohama Fukirō Okura: The Woman Who Moved Politics in the Meiji Era'' (by {{ill|Chōjūmin|ja|鳥居民|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōjūmin|1=Tami Torii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11674923}}) – depicts the relocation festival. * [[Tokyo Woman]] (film) – a black-and-white film released in {{ill|1939|en|1939|qid=Q18639|lt=1939|1=1939}} (Shōwa 14), starring {{ill|Setsuko Hara|en|Setsuko Hara|qid=Q236129|lt=Hara Setsuko|1=Setsuko Hara}}, known as the “Eternal Virgin.” The climactic wedding scene was filmed in front of the worship hall of the shrine. * [[The Nameless, Poor, and Beautiful]] (film) – a black-and-white film released in {{ill|1961|en|1961|qid=Q3696|lt=1961|1=1961}} (Shōwa 36), starring {{ill|Hideko Takamine|en|Hideko Takamine|qid=Q290963|lt=Takamine Hideko|1=Hideko Takamine}}; its wedding scene was shot within the shrine grounds. * [[The World's Most Difficult Love]] (TV drama) – a drama set in Yokohama; in episode 6, during a rock–paper–scissors match, Reiji Samejima (portrayed by {{ill|Satoshi Ohno|en|Satoshi Ohno|qid=Q362271|lt=Ōno Satoshi|1=Satoshi Ohno}}) hands over the shrine’s sacred object to Miura Ieyasu (portrayed by {{ill|Kotaki Nozomi|ja|小瀧望|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotaki Nozomi|1=Nozomu Kotaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10855362}})). * [[We Married as a Job]] (TV drama) – a Yokohama-set drama; episode 10 features a sunrise scene filmed from within the shrine grounds. * ''We Married as a Job: Go for It, Humanity! New Year's Special!!'' (TV drama) – the 2021 ({{ill|2020|en|2020|qid=Q25337|lt=Reiwa 2|1=2020}}) New Year’s special of the above drama; its safe-childbirth prayer scene was filmed in the prayer hall. * {{ill|Kaguya-sama: Love Is War|en|Kaguya-sama: Love Is War|qid=Q24867647|lt=Kaguya-sama: Love Is War|1=Kaguya-sama: Love Is War}} (film) – a theatrical adaptation of the popular manga; an impressive fireworks display scene was filmed in the plaza beside the grand staircase on the approach to the shrine. == Location and Access == 64 Miyazaki Town, Nishi Ward, Yokohama, Kanagawa Prefecture * {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|qid=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Negishi Line|en|Negishi Line|qid=Q1194511|lt=Negishi Line|1=Negishi Line}} and {{ill|Yokohama Municipal Subway|en|Yokohama Municipal Subway|qid=Q1341305|lt=Yokohama Municipal Subway|1=Yokohama Municipal Subway}} {{ill|Blue Line|en|Blue Line|qid=Q162807|lt=Blue Line|1=Blue Line}} – 10-minute walk from {{ill|Sakuragichō Station|en|Sakuragichō Station|qid=Q801396|lt=Sakuragicho Station|1=Sakuragichō Station}}. * {{ill|Keikyu|en|Keikyu|qid=Q1322206|lt=Keikyu Corporation|1=Keikyu}} {{ill|Keikyū Main Line|en|Keikyū Main Line|qid=Q1063632|lt=Main Line|1=Keikyū Main Line}} – 10-minute walk from {{ill|Hinodechō Station|en|Hinodechō Station|qid=Q5767411|lt=Hinodecho Station|1=Hinodechō Station}}. * {{ill|Yokohama Minatomirai Railway|en|Yokohama Minatomirai Railway|qid=Q906967|lt=Yokohama Rapid Transit Railway|1=Yokohama Minatomirai Railway}} {{ill|Minato Mirai Line|ja|みなとみらい線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minato Mirai Line|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 横浜高速鉄道みなとみらい線|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 横浜高速鉄道みなとみらい線|1=Minatomirai Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q852314}} – 15-minute walk from {{ill|Minatomirai Station|en|Minatomirai Station|qid=Q3299269|lt=Minato Mirai Station|1=Minatomirai Station}}. * {{ill|Yokohama Municipal Bus|en|Yokohama Municipal Bus|qid=Q11542999|lt=Yokohama Municipal Bus|1=Yokohama Municipal Bus}} and {{ill|Keihin Kyuko Bus|en|Keihin Kyuko Bus|qid=Q11374532|lt=Keikyu Bus|1=Keihin Kyuko Bus}} – 8-minute walk from the “Kōyōzaka” bus stop. * Parking is available; however, during the first three days of the New Year, traffic restrictions may prevent its use. Note that entry to the shrine grounds is subject to specific opening and closing times. == Footnotes == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == {{commonscat|Iseyama Koutai-jingu}} * {{ill|Hello Kitty|en|Hello Kitty|qid=Q191794|lt=Hello Kitty|1=Hello Kitty}} – An original Hello Kitty charm was created to commemorate the 150th anniversary of Yokohama’s port opening. * {{ill|Gas lighting|en|Gas lighting|qid=Q856548|lt=Gas Lamp|1=Gas lighting}} – The Yokohama Gas Company, which installed Japan’s first gas streetlights, was once located at the foot of Ise Mountain. == External Links == *
